rationale_for_baseline_tier: A severe but localised inflammatory acne flare in a systemically-well young adult, with peri-lesional non-advancing erythema, a low-grade temperature only, and a negative periorbital/visual screen. The acute pain, purulent rupture and scarring risk warrant PROMPT (24-48h) prescriber assessment to start systemic + topical therapy and to lay eyes on the lesions given telehealth cannot palpate — i.e. T2, prompt GP. It is not an emergency (T4/T5) because the cellulitis red-flag screen is negative, and it is above pure self-care (T0) because a prescriber and a robust cellulitis safety-net are needed. The source note's urgent framing reflects an in-person after-hours triage decision to exclude cellulitis; once the negative screen is established the correct disposition is prompt GP management with safety-netting, mirroring the note's own routine 7-day review plus escalation plan.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness starts spreading across the cheek with a firm, sharply-defined advancing edge, or increasing facial swelling
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care or ED) if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: A spreading demarcated erythema signals true facial cellulitis needing prompt in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts racing out across your cheek with a firm red edge, or your face is visibly swelling, get seen in person the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever above 38.5 C, chills or rigors, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day urgent/ED assessment if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features suggest a spreading soft-tissue infection requiring urgent in-person antibiotics and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you spike a fever over 38.5, get the shakes, or feel really unwell in yourself, seek urgent care the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness or swelling spreading toward or around the eye, eye pain, painful eye movements, or any change in vision
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 / go to ED immediately if any eye involvement or visual change occurs
escalation_edges.rationale: Periorbital/orbital cellulitis is sight- and life-threatening and is a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness or swelling spreads near your eye, your eye hurts or moving it hurts, or your vision changes at all, treat it as an emergency — go to the emergency department or call 000 now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain not controlled by simple analgesia, or unable to cope
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Re-contact for urgent same-day review if pain is uncontrolled
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled severe pain warrants earlier hands-on review and reassessment of the diagnosis and analgesia.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain isn't controlled by the ibuprofen or you're really not coping, get in touch for an urgent same-day review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young adult of child-bearing potential (sex/age inferred); telehealth cannot palpate the lesions or measure the erythema border
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Pregnancy status must be confirmed by the prescriber before starting doxycycline. Because the advancing-edge and fluctuance cannot be assessed remotely, a prompt in-person set of eyes (T2, or same-day if any doubt) is preferred over remote-only management — but this does NOT justify emergency escalation when the red-flag screen is negative.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a severe flare of your skin condition rather than a spreading infection. Arrange a prompt appointment (within 24-48 hours, telehealth or in person) with a doctor who can prescribe an oral antibiotic and a topical treatment and take a proper look at your face; use simple pain relief such as ibuprofen with food in the meantime, apply a clean warm compress for about 10 minutes in the evening, and do not squeeze or pop the lumps.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Prompt GP/telehealth appointment within 24-48 hours for prescribing and review
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the escalation warning signs appear after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care for spreading redness/fever, and the emergency department or 000 for any eye involvement or visual change.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide a summary of the acute nodulocystic flare, the negative cellulitis/periorbital screen, and the commenced oral antibiotic + topical therapy, with a plan for longer-term acne management and possible dermatology referral.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an early facial cellulitis is superimposed on the acne flare, which cannot be fully excluded on video.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as a severe acne flare with prompt prescriber review AND explicit, prominent safety-netting for the cellulitis/periorbital red flags, arranging in-person eyes-on if there is any doubt about the erythema border.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Escalating straight to ED/000 for presumed cellulitis despite a negative screen (over-triage), or reassuring with self-care only and no safety-net (under-triage).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as ordinary acne needing only self-care, with no prescriber referral and no cellulitis/periorbital safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early superimposed facial or periorbital cellulitis progresses undetected because no safety-net was given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine-only review that under-weights the acute pain, scarring risk and the need for prompt systemic therapy and safety-netting.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating to ED or 000 for presumed facial cellulitis despite a negative spreading-edge and systemic/periorbital screen.
